llms.summary: The Friendship Practice is a practical friendship coaching website for adults who want to make new friends, reconnect with old friends, and deepen current friendships.
llms.offer: The core offer is a 4-session private coaching package. The public entry point is a free 7-day Friendship Challenge delivered by email.
llms.audience: Adults looking for practical support around friendship, connection, reconnection, and follow-through.

pages.title: How to Make Friends as an Adult | The Friendship Practice
pages.description: The Friendship Practice helps adults make new friends, reconnect with old friends, and deepen current friendships through simple, real-life practice.
pages.ogDescription: A practical friendship coaching practice for adults who want to make new friends, reconnect, and deepen current friendships.

pages.title: 7-Day Friendship Challenge | The Friendship Practice
pages.description: A private, browser-only 7-day friendship challenge to take stock of your friendship life, clarify your principles, and name what friendship practice could mean for you.
pages.ogDescription: A free 7-day challenge to take stock of your friendship life and clarify what friendship practice could mean for you.

guides.title: How to Make Friends as an Adult | The Friendship Practice
guides.description: A practical guide to making friends as an adult, with specific steps for meeting people, following up, and turning early connection into real friendship.
guides.h1: How to make friends as an adult
guides.lead: Making friends as an adult is rarely about being more charismatic. It is usually about being more deliberate: putting yourself near compatible people, following up when something feels warm, and repeating contact before the connection goes cold.
guides.intro: Adult friendship usually feels harder because there is less built-in time, less repetition, and more fear of getting it wrong. But that does not mean friendship is random. It means the practice has to become visible.

sections.h2: Start with places where repetition is normal
paragraphs: The easiest adult friendships usually begin where you see the same people more than once: classes, clubs, sports, volunteering, coworking spaces, recurring neighborhood events, or professional communities with a social edge.

One good conversation matters less than repeated light contact. Familiarity lowers awkwardness. It gives both people time to notice each other, recognize interest, and build trust without forcing intimacy too early.

sections.h2: Treat the follow-up as part of the friendship, not as a test
paragraphs: Many adults assume that if a connection were real, it would continue naturally. In practice, many good beginnings die because no one follows up. A message after meeting is not neediness. It is how adult friendship keeps moving.
Keep the follow-up concrete. Mention something specific you enjoyed, then suggest a small next step: coffee, a walk, another class, a game, a market, a lunch, a check-in next week.

sections.h2: Build from mutual interest, not from perfection
paragraphs: A promising friendship does not need instant closeness. It needs mutual goodwill, enough ease to try again, and enough structure to see each other another time.
If you wait for total certainty, you often miss the moment where friendship could have grown. Adult friendship usually starts as a series of modest invitations, not as a dramatic feeling.
Friendship deepens through repeated acts: inviting, checking in, remembering something small, following through, showing up, and staying in contact after a good interaction.
That is the central idea behind The Friendship Practice: friendship is not just something you understand. It is something you do on purpose.

faq.q: Why is making friends as an adult so hard?
faq.a: It is often harder because adulthood offers less built-in time, less repetition, and more competing responsibilities. The solution is usually not to wait for friendship to happen, but to create more intentional contact and follow-through.

faq.q: How do I turn a good conversation into a friendship?
faq.a: Follow up while the interaction is still warm. Mention something specific you liked, then suggest one small next step. Adult friendship usually grows through repetition, not through one strong first impression.

guides.title: How to Reconnect With Old Friends | The Friendship Practice
guides.description: A practical guide to reconnecting with old friends without overexplaining the distance or waiting for the perfect message.
guides.h1: How to reconnect with old friends
guides.lead: Reconnecting with an old friend is often less complicated than people imagine. The hardest part is usually not the relationship itself. It is the story you tell yourself before you reach out.
guides.intro: Many people wait until they have the perfect explanation for the distance. But reconnection usually starts better with warmth, specificity, and one real invitation than with a long apology letter.

sections.h2: Make the first message simple and specific
paragraphs: A good reconnection message does not need to explain every missing month or year. It can be short: you thought of them, you would love to catch up, and you want to see if they are open to it.
Specificity helps. Mention a memory, a place, a habit you shared, or something that made you think of them. It reminds the other person that the relationship was real.

sections.h2: Do not turn the message into a guilt performance
paragraphs: A brief acknowledgment of the distance can be kind. A long self-accusing message can put pressure on the other person to comfort you before the reconnection even begins.
The goal is not to prove that you regret losing touch. The goal is to reopen contact with enough lightness that a real conversation can happen.
If the friend responds warmly, make the next move easy: propose a call, coffee, lunch, a walk, or a video chat if you live far apart.
Reconnection becomes real through a specific plan. Without one, warm intention can drift back into silence.

sections.h2: Accept that not every reconnection will become close again
paragraphs: Some old friendships return quickly. Others stay fond but light. Some have reached their natural end. Reaching out is still worthwhile because it replaces uncertainty with reality.
The practice is not controlling the outcome. The practice is making the attempt honestly and giving the relationship a chance.

faq.q: What do I say to an old friend after a long time?
faq.a: Keep it simple and specific. Say you thought of them, mention something real you remember or appreciate, and ask if they would like to catch up.

faq.q: Do I need to apologize for losing touch?
faq.a: A brief acknowledgment can help, but a long apology is usually not necessary. What matters most is warmth, clarity, and a concrete next step.

guides.title: Why Making Friends as an Adult Is Hard | The Friendship Practice
guides.description: Why adult friendship can feel so difficult, and what changes when you stop treating friendship as luck and start treating it as practice.
guides.h1: Why making friends as an adult is hard
guides.lead: Adult friendship often feels hard for structural reasons, not because you are uniquely bad at it. The conditions that made friendship easy when you were younger are often missing now.
guides.intro: As children, teenagers, and students, friendship is supported by repetition, proximity, and shared time. As adults, we are expected to build intimacy in small windows between work, logistics, relationships, and fatigue.

paragraphs: Friendship grows through seeing people again. School, campus life, and early jobs often create repeated contact automatically. Later in life, those structures are weaker or disappear altogether.
When repetition disappears, friendship requires more intention. Someone has to suggest the next coffee, the next walk, the next plan.

sections.h2: Many adults are afraid of seeming awkward, eager, or needy
paragraphs: A lot of adult friendship stalls not because there is no interest, but because both people are waiting for the other person to move first. They are protecting themselves from rejection, but they are also preventing momentum.
This is why practical friendship support matters. The issue is often not insight. It is follow-through.
Jobs, commutes, children, partners, travel, caregiving, exhaustion, and digital distraction all compete with friendship. Without practice, closeness gets treated like a bonus instead of a priority.
That does not mean friendship is impossible. It means it needs time, planning, and repeated action to stay alive.

sections.h2: The answer is not to force it. It is to practice it.
paragraphs: You do not need a perfect personality to build adult friendship. You need a few repeatable behaviors: noticing mutual interest, following up, inviting, reconnecting, and staying present over time.
This is the shift The Friendship Practice is built around: friendship is not a miracle that happens to lucky people. It is a set of actions that can be practiced in real life.

faq.q: Is it normal to struggle with friendship as an adult?
faq.a: Yes. Many adults struggle because there is less built-in time, less repetition, and more fear around initiating contact. The difficulty is common, even if people do not talk about it openly.

faq.q: Can adult friendship actually get easier?
faq.a: Yes, usually when you stop waiting for friendship to happen by itself and start practicing specific behaviors like following up, inviting, and reconnecting.
